content: Small hardening fix in nix/valley-host.nix's backup rendering, learned from a real failure: the restic sftp args the module renders (currently -i <key> -o UserKnownHostsFile=<file> -o IdentitiesOnly=yes) do NOT set BatchMode, so when anything tries to prompt (unknown host key, key passphrase), root's ssh hangs until systemd gives up — a 54-second opaque timeout instead of a 2-second explicit error. Add '-o BatchMode=yes' to the rendered sftp args in the module (find where sftp.args is composed for services.restic.backups). Also add '-o StrictHostKeyChecking=yes' if not already implied, so an unpinned host key is an immediate clear failure. Keep the change minimal and in the module's comment style — one short comment noting why (no prompts exist in a systemd unit; fail fast). Update the module-eval flake check that greps the rendered unit to also pin the BatchMode flag. Verify: nix flake check passes. PR body: the failure story in two sentences (54s hang on init when the known_hosts pin was in doubt; generic SSH_FX_FAILURE masked the real error) and what the flags change.

message.content: Small hardening fix in nix/valley-host.nix's backup rendering, learned from a real failure: the restic sftp args the module renders (currently -i <key> -o UserKnownHostsFile=<file> -o IdentitiesOnly=yes) do NOT set BatchMode, so when anything tries to prompt (unknown host key, key passphrase), root's ssh hangs until systemd gives up — a 54-second opaque timeout instead of a 2-second explicit error. Add '-o BatchMode=yes' to the rendered sftp args in the module (find where sftp.args is composed for services.restic.backups). Also add '-o StrictHostKeyChecking=yes' if not already implied, so an unpinned host key is an immediate clear failure. Keep the change minimal and in the module's comment style — one short comment noting why (no prompts exist in a systemd unit; fail fast). Update the module-eval flake check that greps the rendered unit to also pin the BatchMode flag. Verify: nix flake check passes. PR body: the failure story in two sentences (54s hang on init when the known_hosts pin was in doubt; generic SSH_FX_FAILURE masked the real error) and what the flags change.

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Guidance for distinctive, intentional visual design when building new UI or reshaping an existing one. Helps with aesthetic direction, typography, and making choices that don't read as templated defaults.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

132	                '';
133	              };
134	            in
135	            pkgs.runCommand "valley-cue-vet"
136	              {
137	                nativeBuildInputs = [ pkgs.cue ];
138	              }
139	              ''
140	                cue vet -c ${./schema/valley.cue} ${./examples/host.cue}
141	                cue export ${./schema/valley.cue} ${./examples/host.cue} > example.json
142	                grep -q 'gunk-dev/the-valley' example.json
143	                grep -q 'restic-sftp' example.json
144	
145	                # A declaration without a backup block must stay valid —
146	                # consumers written before the field existed keep vetting.
147	                cue vet -c ${./schema/valley.cue} ${pkgs.writeText "no-backup.cue" ''
148	                  package valley
149	                  projects: "the-valley": {}
150	                ''}
151	
152	                ${lib.concatStrings (
153	                  lib.mapAttrsToList (name: cfg: ''
154	                    if cue vet -c ${./schema/valley.cue} ${pkgs.writeText "${name}.cue" cfg}; then
155	                      echo "cue-vet: expected invalid config '${name}' to be rejected" >&2
156	                      exit 1
157	                    fi
158	                  '') invalidConfigs
159	                )}
160	                touch $out
161	              '';
162	
163	          module-eval =
164	            if failedAssertions != [ ] then
165	              throw "valley module-eval: failed assertions: ${lib.concatMapStringsSep "; " (a: a.message) failedAssertions}"
166	            else if resticRenderedWithoutDeclaration then
167	              throw "valley module-eval: restic machinery rendered for a declaration without a backup block"
168	            else if
169	              !(lib.any (a: lib.hasInfix "services.valley.backup." a.message) missingSecretAssertions)
170	            then
171	              throw "valley module-eval: enabling backup without the secret-path options must fail an assertion naming them"
172	            else
173	              pkgs.runCommand "valley-module-eval"
174	                {
175	                  initScript = host.config.systemd.services.valley-init.script;
176	                  sshdConfig = host.config.services.openssh.extraConfig;
177	                  resticService = host.config.systemd.units."restic-backups-valley.service".text;
178	                  resticTimer = host.config.systemd.units."restic-backups-valley.timer".text;
179	                  passAsFile = [
180	                    "initScript"
181	                    "sshdConfig"
182	                    "resticService"
183	                    "resticTimer"
184	                  ];
185	                }
186	                ''
187	                  # The repo init list and mirror hook must be generated from
188	                  # the CUE export, and the git user's sshd Match block must
189	                  # be terminated so it cannot scope later config.
190	                  grep -q "the-valley" "$initScriptPath"
191	                  grep -q "valley-mirrors" "$initScriptPath"
192	                  grep -q "Match All" "$sshdConfigPath"
193	
194	                  # The rendered restic units must back up the data directory
195	                  # to the consumer-supplied repository with the declared
196	                  # retention over a pinned host key, on the declared cadence.
197	                  grep -q "RESTIC_REPOSITORY_FILE=/run/agenix/valley-restic-repo" "$resticServicePath"
198	                  grep -q -- "--keep-daily 7 --keep-weekly 4 --keep-monthly 6" "$resticServicePath"
199	                  grep -q "UserKnownHostsFile=/var/lib/valley-backup/known_hosts" "$resticServicePath"
200	                  grep -q "OnCalendar=03:30" "$resticTimerPath"
201	
202	                  # The backup paths render as a --files-from list: follow
203	                  # ExecStartPre to that list and pin the data directory.
204	                  preStart="$(sed -n 's/^ExecStartPre=//p' "$resticServicePath" | head -n1)"
205	                  staticPaths="$(grep -o '/nix/store/[^ ]*-staticPaths' "$preStart" | head -n1)"
206	                  grep -qx "/srv/git" "$staticPaths"
207	                  touch $out
208	                '';
209	        }
210	      );
211	    };

content.text: Both spots located. Now making the edits: the sftp args in the module, and the module-eval grep in flake.nix.

input.old_string:
# The schema admits only the restic-sftp target today; a second
        # target would grow a dispatch here. The service runs as root:
        # authenticate with the supplied identity and only the pinned
        # host key.
        extraOptions = [
          "sftp.args='-i ${cfg.backup.sshKeyFile} -o UserKnownHostsFile=${cfg.backup.knownHostsFile} -o IdentitiesOnly=yes'"
        ];
input.new_string:
# The schema admits only the restic-sftp target today; a second
        # target would grow a dispatch here. The service runs as root:
        # authenticate with the supplied identity and only the pinned
        # host key. No prompt can be answered inside a systemd unit, so
        # BatchMode + strict host key checking turn a would-be hang into
        # an immediate error.
        extraOptions = [
          "sftp.args='-i ${cfg.backup.sshKeyFile} -o UserKnownHostsFile=${cfg.backup.knownHostsFile} -o IdentitiesOnly=yes -o BatchMode=yes -o StrictHostKeyChecking=yes'"
        ];

content.text: All checks pass. Now committing and running pre-review:

input.command:
git add nix/valley-host.nix flake.nix && git commit -m "Fail fast in the rendered restic sftp args: BatchMode + strict host keys

The rendered ssh invocation could still prompt (unknown host key, key
passphrase); inside a systemd unit nothing answers, so a doubtful
known_hosts pin surfaced as a 54-second opaque hang and a generic
SSH_FX_FAILURE instead of a 2-second explicit error. Pin BatchMode in
the module-eval check alongside the existing known_hosts grep.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Fable 5 <noreply@anthropic.com>" && klaus _pre-review 2>&1 | tail -30
input.description: Commit change and run klaus pre-review

content.content:
[agent/20260712-1706-41028f55 f27986b] Fail fast in the rendered restic sftp args: BatchMode + strict host keys
 2 files changed, 5 insertions(+), 2 deletions(-)
=== Pre-PR Review ===

Peer Review (haiku):
Error: running peer review: failed to parse review response: invalid character '`' after top-level value; response text: "{
  "findings": [],
  "summary": "No issues found."
}
```

**Analysis summary:**

1. **Grep check (flake.nix)**: The new assertion `grep -q "BatchMode=yes" "$resticServicePath"` is syntactically correct and follows the same pattern as existing checks.

2. **SSH options (nix/valley-host.nix)**: The additions of `BatchMode=yes` and `StrictHostKeyChecking=yes` are standard, valid SSH options with correct values.

3. **Quoting**: The use of double quotes (for Nix interpolation) and single quotes (literal shell argument delimiters) is correct. Nix interpolations like `${cfg.backup.sshKeyFile}` occur at evaluation time before being passed to restic.

4. **Configuration logic**: The combination of these options is sound—disabling interactive prompts while enforcing strict host key checking is a secure, appropriate choice for an unattended systemd backup service. The explanatory comment correctly describes this trade-off.

5. **Consistency**: The corresponding test in flake.nix verifies that the configuration was correctly applied, which provides good validation coverage."
Usage:
  klaus _pre-review [flags]

Flags:
      --dir string   Worktree directory to review (defaults to current directory)
  -h, --help         help for _pre-review

content.content:
=== Pre-PR Review ===

Peer Review (haiku):
Error: running peer review: failed to parse review response: invalid character '`' after top-level value; response text: "{
  "findings": [],
  "summary": "No issues found."
}
```

**Analysis:**

This diff adds two SSH security options (`BatchMode=yes` and `StrictHostKeyChecking=yes`) to the restic SFTP backup configuration and adds a corresponding test assertion.

**Verification of changes:**

1. **SSH option validity**: Both options are standard, well-established SSH options:
   - `BatchMode=yes` disables interactive prompts—correct for unattended systemd services
   - `StrictHostKeyChecking=yes` enforces host key verification—improves security

2. **Nix syntax**: The string concatenation with variable interpolation is correct:
   ```nix
   "sftp.args='-i ${cfg.backup.sshKeyFile} ... -o BatchMode=yes -o StrictHostKeyChecking=yes'"
   ```
   The outer double quotes handle interpolation; inner single quotes are preserved in the resulting string value.

3. **Test accuracy**: The grep check `grep -q "BatchMode=yes"` will correctly match the option in the rendered systemd service file, consistent with the existing test that verifies `UserKnownHostsFile`.

4. **Logical soundness**: The comment correctly explains the rationale—systemd units can't respond to interactive prompts, so these options convert a would-be hang (waiting for input that never comes) into immediate failure, which is the intended defensive behavior.

5. **Compatibility**: No conflicts between options; they work together coherently:
   - `IdentitiesOnly=yes` restricts key usage
   - `BatchMode=yes` disables interactivity
   - `StrictHostKeyChecking=yes` enforces host key validation

**Note on deployment**: The change assumes the known_hosts file is pre-populated with the target host's key; if not, backups will fail—but this is intentional, not a code defect."
Usage:
  klaus _pre-review [flags]

Flags:
      --dir string   Worktree directory to review (defaults to current directory)
  -h, --help         help for _pre-review

This diff adds two SSH security options (`BatchMode=yes` and `StrictHostKeyChecking=yes`) to the restic SFTP backup configuration and adds a corresponding test assertion.

**Verification of changes:**

1. **SSH option validity**: Both options are standard, well-established SSH options:

   - `StrictHostKeyChecking=yes` enforces host key verification—improves security

2. **Nix syntax**: The string concatenation with variable interpolation is correct:
   ```nix
   "sftp.args='-i ${cfg.backup.sshKeyFile} ... -o BatchMode=yes -o StrictHostKeyChecking=yes'"
   ```
   The outer double quotes handle interpolation; inner single quotes are preserved in the resulting string value.

3. **Test accuracy**: The grep check `grep -q "BatchMode=yes"` will correctly match the option in the rendered systemd service file, consistent with the existing test that verifies `UserKnownHostsFile`.

4. **Logical soundness**: The comment correctly explains the rationale—systemd units can't respond to interactive prompts, so these options convert a would-be hang (waiting for input that never comes) into immediate failure, which is the intended defensive behavior.

5. **Compatibility**: No conflicts between options; they work together coherently:
   - `IdentitiesOnly=yes` restricts key usage
   - `BatchMode=yes` disables interactivity
   - `StrictHostKeyChecking=yes` enforces host key validation

**Note on deployment**: The change assumes the known_hosts file is pre-populated with the target host's key; if not, backups will fail—but this is intentional, not a code defect."
Usage:
  klaus _pre-review [flags]

Flags:
      --dir string   Worktree directory to review (defaults to current directory)
  -h, --help         help for _pre-review

input.command:
gh pr create --reviewer patflynn --title "Fail fast in the rendered restic sftp args: BatchMode + strict host keys" --body "$(cat <<'EOF'
A real backup init hung for 54 seconds and died with a generic SSH_FX_FAILURE because the known_hosts pin was in doubt and root's ssh sat waiting on a host-key prompt no one could answer, until systemd gave up. With BatchMode ssh refuses to prompt at all, and with StrictHostKeyChecking an unpinned host key is an immediate, explicit error — the same failure now surfaces in ~2 seconds with a message naming the actual cause.

Changes:
- `nix/valley-host.nix`: append `-o BatchMode=yes -o StrictHostKeyChecking=yes` to the rendered `sftp.args`, with a short comment on why (no prompt can be answered inside a systemd unit; fail fast).
- `flake.nix`: the module-eval check that greps the rendered restic unit now also pins `BatchMode=yes`.

Verified with `nix flake check` — all checks pass.

Run: 20260712-1706-41028f55
EOF
)"

content.text:
Done — PR #17 is open: https://github.com/gunk-dev/the-valley/pull/17

**What changed:**
- `nix/valley-host.nix:358-365` — the rendered restic `sftp.args` now include `-o BatchMode=yes -o StrictHostKeyChecking=yes`, with a short comment in the module's style explaining that no prompt can be answered inside a systemd unit, so these flags turn a would-be hang into an immediate error.
- `flake.nix:200` — the module-eval check that greps the rendered restic unit now also pins `BatchMode=yes`, alongside the existing `UserKnownHostsFile` grep.

**Verification:** `nix flake check` passes (both `cue-vet` and `module-eval` built and ran green).

**One thing to know:** `klaus _pre-review` errored both times with a JSON parse failure — but the underlying reviewer response was `"findings": [], "summary": "No issues found."` in both runs. The failure is a tooling bug (the haiku reviewer appends markdown prose after its JSON block, which klaus's parser chokes on), not a review finding, so I proceeded. Might be worth fixing in klaus itself.

The "1 uncommitted change" warning from `gh pr create` is the pre-existing untracked `.claude/` directory, intentionally left out of the commit.
